topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed, a grating soundtrack to my internal turmoil. I stared at the printed accusation, the harsh black ink seeming to burn into my vision. My hands, usually steady when writing, trembled slightly as I reread the cited passages. Every sentence felt like a trap sprung, the borrowed words suddenly feeling alien, like wearing someone else's ill-fitting skin. I wanted to sink through the floorboards, to disappear into the stacks of forgotten texts. When Professor Albright called my name, the sound echoed disproportionately loud in the silence. My throat tightened, and I could only manage a shallow, breathy murmur before shuffling toward the office, each step heavy with unspoken dread.

[story 2]

The silence in my dorm room was oppressive, thick enough to choke on. I kept replaying the moments leading up to the submission, the frantic late-night scrolling through sources, the way the ideas had simply flowed—or perhaps, were copied—into my own work. A cold sweat prickled my hairline despite the warmth of the room. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool against my fingertips, and found the ink refused to flow smoothly. I chewed on the end of the pen, the dry taste sharp on my tongue. When the email arrived, the screen seemed to swim. I didn't look at the words immediately; instead, I focused intently on the pattern of the wood grain on my desk, trying to anchor myself against the rising tide of panic that threatened to pull me under.

[story 3]

The accusation hung in the air like dust motes caught in a single shaft of sunlight. I stood by the window, watching the campus quad move below, the vibrant energy of other students a stark contrast to the paralyzing stillness inside me. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. Every time I tried to articulate a defense, the words caught in my throat, dissolving into a dry rasp. I felt a profound sense of wrongness, a sickening lurch in my stomach that made breathing shallow. When the department head finally spoke, their measured tone felt like a physical weight pressing down on my chest, making it hard to draw a full, steady breath.
